story.heading: Search Continues for Nancy Guthrie in Arizona Catalina Foothills
story.summary: Authorities continue to search for 84-year-old Nancy Guthrie, mother of NBC's Savannah Guthrie, who was last seen Jan. 31 at her Catalina Foothills home. Investigators are reviewing video footage, DNA results and neighborhood leads while officials say the family is not considered a suspect.

segments.heading: Disappearance and initial search
segments.text: Nancy Guthrie, 84, was last seen on January 31 at her secluded home in the Catalina Foothills. Authorities say she disappeared from the residence without her phone or required medications, and the case has prompted an intensive, ongoing search and public appeals for information.

citations.quote: Nancy Guthrie was last seen on January 31, before she was apparently kidnapped, disappearing from her secluded home in Arizonas Catalina Foothills without her phone  or critical medications.

segments.heading: Evidence and investigative activity
segments.text: Investigators have reviewed video footage and physical evidence from the area. Law enforcement described video showing a masked person tampering with the home's doorbell camera and noted the individual carried an Ozark backpack. DNA testing on gloves found roughly two miles from the home did not match national databases or DNA recovered inside the house. Authorities and the FBI are canvassing the neighborhood and reviewing thousands of hours of additional surveillance video.

citations.quote: video of an armed, masked person tampering with the doorbell camera at her Tucson home.
DNA on gloves found 2 miles from the home did not return any matches in the national database known as CODIS, and doesnt match DNA found the house, either, Nanos said.
NEIGHBOR EXCLUSIVE: Nancy Guthrie neighbors' Ring camera captures vehicles on possible route from crime scene

segments.heading: Neighborhood leads and public response
segments.text: Neighbors have reported suspicious activity and some residents have shared tips and footage with investigators. Media accounts describe local online communities exchanging theories and leads, with some people organizing informal searches and tip-sharing.

citations.quote: FOOTHILLS PHANTOM: Nancy Guthrie's neighbor saw suspicious man walking nearby 2 weeks before suspected abduction
DIGITAL VIGILANTES: Nancy Guthrie disappearance fuels rise of mom detectives swapping tips and losing sleep

segments.heading: Family status and official statements
segments.text: Authorities have said Guthrie's adult children and their spouses are not considered suspects and cautioned against suggesting otherwise. Officials have declined to release some investigative details as the probe continues.

citations.quote: Nancy Guthries adult children and their spouses are not suspects in her February 1 disappearance, the sheriff says, calling any suggestion otherwise cruel.

Detectives and agents were back in Nancy Guthries neighborhood on Saturday canvassing the area as part of their investigation, according to the Pima County Sheriffs Department. No additional details will be provided, the agency said.

segments.heading: Cross-border checks and outstanding questions
segments.text: Mexican officials have said there is currently no indication Guthrie was taken into Mexico, and investigators have not announced a leading theory about motive. Authorities have not ruled out the involvement of more than one person, and several investigative avenues remain open.

citations.quote: Mexican officials say there is currently no indication Guthrie was brought into Mexico.
They have not ruled out the possibility more than one person may have been involved, the sheriff said Friday.

segments.heading: How the public can provide information
segments.text: Authorities have asked anyone with information to contact investigative tip lines or submit information to official channels.

citations.quote: If you have information that could help investigators, you can call 1-800-CALL-FBI or 520-351-4900. You can also submit information at tips.fbi.gov.

story.heading: Final Day Schedule and Key Events at the 2026 Milano Cortina Winter Olympics
story.summary: The Milano Cortina Games conclude with a set of medal-deciding events and the closing ceremony. Broadcast coverage continues across NBC networks and Peacock; highlights on the final competition day include the men’s ice hockey final, women’s freestyle halfpipe, a historic 50km women’s cross-country race and medal rounds in bobsled and curling.

segments.heading: Broadcast and coverage
segments.text: NBC is handling the primary U.S. broadcast coverage for the final competition day, with replays and highlights airing on its linear networks and live competitions available on the Peacock streaming service.

citations.quote: The broadcast coverage of the 2026 Milano Cortina Winter Olympics continues on Wednesday, Feb. 18 with NBC airing a number of replays featuring Team USA's athletes and the day's biggest moments.
The games are exclusively airing across NBC's suite of networks with many competitions airing live on its streaming service, Peacock, which you can sign up for here .

segments.heading: Men’s ice hockey final: United States vs. Canada
segments.text: The men’s gold medal game pits the United States against Canada. The match is scheduled for the final competition day and will be available on Peacock. Prior women’s finals between the two nations drew large television audiences in the U.S., a point of context for expected viewership.

citations.quote:
Ice hockey: United States vs. Canada in mens gold medal game

Times: 8:10 a.m. ET, 2:10 p.m. in Italy

TV: Peacock
The womens hockey gold medal game between these same two nations had the largest U.S. TV audience for a womens hockey game on record, with an average of 5.3 million viewers.

segments.heading: Freestyle skiing — Women’s halfpipe
segments.text: The women’s halfpipe final, delayed by weather, features defending champion Eileen Gu among the finalists. Gu, who represents China, qualified fifth for the final and is aiming to add to her previous Olympic medals.

citations.quote: What to watch for: Snow in Livigno led to the postponement of the final scheduled for Saturday night.
Eileen Gu, the 22-year-old who was born in San Francisco, attends Stanford and competes for China, is the defending gold medalist and qualified fifth for the final.

segments.heading: Cross-country skiing — Historic women’s 50-kilometre race
segments.text: For the first time in Olympic history women are competing in a 50km cross-country event at the Winter Games. The distance is longer than a marathon and is part of the IOCs effort to increase gender balance across event programs. The race marks the final Olympic appearance for U.S. skier Jessie Diggins.

citations.quote: This is the first time women will compete in the Olympics at 50km  a staple distance for the men  in support of making the 2026 Games what the International Olympic Committee has called the most gender-balanced Olympic Winter Games in history.
The race will be the final Olympic appearance for the United States Jessie Diggins, who earned silver in 2022 in the 30km freestyle, which was the longest womens race at the time.

segments.heading: Bobsled and curling medal rounds
segments.text: In four-man bobsled competition, German crews led after early heats while the top U.S. sled sat outside medal contention through two heats. The womens curling final features Sweden and Switzerland, with Sweden bidding for another Olympic medal and Switzerland seeking its first Olympic gold in the event.

citations.quote: German teams have the three fastest combined times through two heats.

Curling: Sweden vs. Switzerland in womens gold medal game

What to watch for: Sweden will collect its sixth consecutive Olympic medal and is hunting for its fourth gold in the last six Olympics.

segments.heading: Closing day context and notable moments from the Games
segments.text: The final day concludes a Games that produced a range of memorable and unusual moments alongside the sporting competition. The day wraps up with medal finals and the closing ceremony.

citations.quote: Well, this is it. The Milan Cortina Olympics wrap up Sunday with five events, all of which determine gold medals, followed by the closing ceremony.

Among them: a dog loping down the homestretch during the womens cross-country skiing team sprint qualifying, the biathlete who confessed on television to cheating on his girlfriend and a skier who walked off into the woods after missing a gate on his final run.

story.heading: Pakistan carries out airstrikes on Afghan border targets; Afghanistan reports civilian casualties
story.summary: Pakistan says its military conducted "intelligence-based, selective operations" against militant camps in Afghanistan after a string of deadly attacks in Pakistan. Afghan authorities called the strikes a violation of sovereignty and reported civilian casualties, including children; casualty figures and some details differ between Pakistani and Afghan accounts.

segments.heading: What Pakistan says it did and why
segments.text: Pakistan's information ministry said the military carried out targeted airstrikes against militant hideouts across the border in Afghanistan, describing the operations as "intelligence-based, selective" and saying they struck seven camps belonging to the Pakistani Taliban (TTP), affiliated groups and an Islamic State-linked group. Islamabad framed the strikes as a response to a recent series of deadly attacks inside Pakistan and said it had evidence linking those attacks to militants with Afghanistan-based leadership and handlers.

citations.quote:
Pakistanederal
nd provincial information ministries said its military conducted intelligence-based, selective operations against seven camps belonging to militants it blames for a recent series of deadly attacks on its soil.
Tarar said Pakistan had conclusive evidence that the recent attacks ... were carried out by militants acting at the behest of their Afghanistan-based leadership and handlers.

segments.heading: Afghan response and reported civilian toll
segments.text: Afghanistan's Ministry of Defense confirmed strikes inside Afghan territory and described them as a breach of sovereignty and international law. Afghan officials and state media reported civilian casualties; accounts vary, with an Afghan police official cited by CNN saying 18 people were killed, including women and children, while The Guardian reported that Afghan authorities said children were among dozens killed and wounded.

citations.quote:
Afghanistan
efense ministry confirmed the strikes in a statement, calling them a blatant violation of Afghanistans national sovereignty and a clear breach of international law.
Women and children were among the 18 people killed, Sayed Tayeb Hamad, a senior police official in Nangarhar, said on Afghan state television.
where the government reported children were among dozens of people killed and wounded.

segments.heading: Details, limits and competing accounts
segments.text: Pakistan described the operations as limited and intelligence-driven but did not provide precise strike locations; Afghan statements said the strikes hit civilian areas in the eastern provinces of Nangarhar and Paktika and damaged homes and a religious seminary. The two sides' accounts differ on the scale of casualties and the characterization of targets, with Kabul calling the strikes a violation of sovereignty and Islamabad saying it was targeting militant camps responsible for attacks inside Pakistan.

citations.quote: Islamabad did not say precisely where the strikes were carried out or provide other details.
The strikes took place in civilian areas in the eastern Afghan provinces of Nangarhar and Paktika, targeting a religious seminary and multiple civilian homes, the ministry said.

segments.heading: Context and potential implications
segments.text: Analysts and officials view the strikes against the backdrop of rising militant attacks in Pakistan in recent weeks and a fragile, Qatar-mediated ceasefire between Islamabad and Kabul since October. Pakistan has warned it would not "exercise any restraint" in pursuing those it holds responsible, and the latest action will test the ceasefire and relations between the neighbouring countries, which have experienced deadly border clashes in recent months.

citations.quote: After Saturdays violence, Pakistans military warned it would not exercise any restraint and operations against those responsible would continue irrespective of their location.
This new escalation will test the delicate ceasefire that has been in place between the neighboring countries since last October, after they traded their deadliest fire in years.

story.heading: Middle Eastern and Muslim-majority states condemn US ambassador's comments supporting broad Israeli territorial claims
story.summary: US ambassador to Israel Mike Huckabee told an interview that "it would be fine" if Israel took control of a large swathe of territory referenced in a biblical passage. His remarks prompted a joint denunciation from multiple Arab and Islamic states and responses from individual governments, while US officials were sought for comment.

segments.heading: Huckabee's remarks in recorded interview
segments.text: In an interview on Tucker Carlson's podcast, US ambassador to Israel Mike Huckabee discussed a biblical passage sometimes interpreted as extending Israel's borders to lands between the Nile and the Euphrates. When pressed on whether Israel had a right to that territory, Huckabee said "it would be fine if they took it all," and described the idea elsewhere in the exchange as a large or "hyperbolic" claim rather than a detailed policy proposal.

citations.quote: "It would be fine if they took it all," Huckabee responded
he continued that Israel was "not asking to take all of that", adding: "It was somewhat of a hyperbolic statement."

segments.heading: Joint condemnation by Arab and Islamic states
segments.text: A joint statement led by the United Arab Emirates and supported by several Arab and Muslim-majority countries denounced Huckabee's comments, calling them inflammatory and contrary to international law. The statement was issued by multiple states and regional organisations.

citations.quote: A statement led by the United Arab Emirates and backed by Saudi Arabia, Egypt, Jordan, Qatar and others denounced Huckabee's comments as "dangerous and inflammatory."

was signed by the UAE, Egypt, Jordan, Indonesia, Pakistan, Turkey, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Kuwait, Oman, Bahrain, Lebanon, Syria and the State of Palestine, as well as the Organization of Islamic Cooperation, the Arab League and the Gulf Cooperation Council.

segments.heading: Individual government reactions and legal concerns
segments.text: Several governments issued specific condemnations, describing the comments as violations of international law, threats to regional sovereignty, or likely to inflame violence. Saudi Arabia called the remarks "reckless" and "irresponsible," Jordan termed them "an assault on the sovereignty of the countries of the region," and Egypt reaffirmed that Israel has no sovereignty over occupied Palestinian territory or other Arab lands. Palestinian and other officials said the remarks contradict stated US positions against annexation of occupied territory.

citations.quote: Saudi Arabia described the ambassador's words as "reckless" and "irresponsible", while Jordan said it was "an assault on the sovereignty of the countries of the region".
the comments were a "flagrant violation of international law"

segments.heading: Context: Huckabee's previous statements and US policy
segments.text: The remarks came amid existing debate over annexation of occupied territories. After his nomination as ambassador, Huckabee had said he supported Israeli annexation of the occupied West Bank and pledged to carry out presidential policy. President Donald Trump has previously said he would not allow Israel to annex the West Bank. Huckabee later posted clarifications on X but did not directly address the biblical-border remark.

citations.quote: shortly after Trump announced Huckabee as his pick for ambassador to Israel, Huckabee made clear that he supports Israel's annexation of the occupied West Bank.
But last September Trump said: "I will not allow Israel to annex the West Bank. Nope, I will not allow it. It's not going to happen."
On Saturday, Huckabee published two posts on X further clarifying his position on other topics touched upon in the interview, but did not address his remark about the biblical verse.

segments.heading: Requests for US government comment
segments.text: News organisations sought comment from US officials about the ambassador's remarks and the regional responses.

citations.quote: CNN has reached out to the State Department for comment on Huckabee's remarks.

story.heading: Trump Announces Increase of Global Tariffs to 15% After Supreme Court Ruling
story.summary: President Donald Trump said he would raise a previously announced 10% global tariff to 15% effective immediately, citing alternative trade authorities after the US Supreme Court limited his prior use of emergency powers. The move and its timing have drawn legal, economic and international responses while questions remain about implementation and refunds.

segments.heading: What the administration announced
segments.text: President Trump announced on social media that he would raise a temporary global tariff rate from 10% to 15% and said the change would be effective immediately. The announcement followed the administration’s more recent statement that it would impose a 10% worldwide tariff under a different legal authority.

citations.quote: I, as President of the United States of America, will be, effective immediately, raising the 10% Worldwide Tariff on Countries, many of which have been ‘ripping’ the U.S. off for decades, without retribution (until I came along!), to the fully allowed, and legally tested, 15% level.
President Donald Trump said on Saturday that he will increase the global tariffs he imposed a day earlier to 15% from 10% following an adverse ruling at the Supreme Court.

segments.heading: Legal basis and recent court decision
segments.text: The Supreme Court ruled that the prior use of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) to impose broad tariffs exceeded the president’s authority. Administration officials have pointed to Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974 as an alternative legal route that allows a temporary tariff of up to 15% for 150 days, subject to subsequent congressional approval.

citations.quote: The court on Friday ruled that Trump exceeded his authority with the way he imposed tariffs on trading partners using the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), a law from 1977 reserved for national emergencies.
The law according to section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974  which has never been used  allows the president to impose a levy of up to 15% for 150 days, although it could face legal challenges.

segments.heading: Scope, exemptions and other trade authorities
segments.text: The announced temporary 15% rate is described as applying to imports from all countries, with some product exemptions noted. Separately, the administration has used and could use other statutory authorities to impose industry-specific duties.

citations.quote: Some products will be exempted from the temporary tariffs including critical minerals, metals and pharmaceuticals. Other exemptions include USMCA-compliant goods from Canada and Mexico.
The administration has already used Section 232 investigations to impose across-the-board duties on steel, aluminum, copper, lumber, furniture, cars and car parts.

The administration could also use Section 301 to investigate countries that potentially violate other nations trade agreements or practices in a way that is 'unjustifiable' and 'burdens or restricts' US business.

segments.heading: Economic effects and reactions
segments.text: Analysts and officials said effects would vary by country and sector. Some countries that had been subject to higher, industry-specific rates would face lower levies under a uniform 15% rate, while other countries that had been spared under earlier arrangements could face higher duties. Business groups have sought refunds of tariffs collected under the IEEPA ruling, and European leaders urged an end to tariff-related uncertainty.

citations.quote: The 15% duties would make this years effective tariff rate 6%, according to Erica York, vice president of federal tax policy at the Tax Foundation.
For many countries, a 15% duty is better than the rates faced under the IEEPA tariffs.
The US Chamber of Commerce, too, called for swift return of an estimated $133bn in collected tariffs covered by the ruling.
The biggest poison for the economies of Europe and the US is this constant uncertainty about tariffs. And this uncertainty must end,

segments.heading: Timing and implementation questions
segments.text: Although the president said the increase would be effective immediately, published reports noted uncertainty about whether required official documents had been signed and when changes would take effect. Officials and analysts also flagged questions about eligibility for refunds of duties already collected.

citations.quote: While Trumps announcement claimed that the new tariffs would take effect 'immediately,' it was unclear whether any official documents had been signed confirming the timing.
Consumers who paid higher prices on many items may not benefit from lower tariffs, and theres a lack of certainty about refunds for individual importers.

story.heading: Ukraine Condemns Hungary and Slovakia Over Threats to Cut Emergency Electricity Supplies
story.summary: Ukraine's foreign ministry called recent demands from Hungary and Slovakia 'ultimatums and blackmail' after the two neighbours threatened to halt emergency electricity exports unless Kyiv restarts transit of Russian oil. The dispute follows a January pipeline outage and occurs amid continued strikes on Ukraine's energy infrastructure.

segments.heading: Dispute over energy supplies
segments.text: Ukraine's foreign ministry publicly rejected and condemned what it described as ultimatums and blackmail from Hungary and Slovakia after both governments said they would stop emergency electricity supplies unless Kyiv resumed transit of Russian oil. Slovakia and Hungary say Ukraine is responsible for the prolonged outage of oil shipments; Kyiv disputes that and urged that demands be directed at Russia.

citations.quote: Ukraine rejects and condemns the ultimatums and blackmail by the governments of Hungary and the Slovak Republic regarding energy supplies between our countries
Slovakia and Hungary both accuse Ukraine of delaying the restart, without evidence.

segments.heading: Cause and transit options for oil shipments
segments.text: Shipments of Russian oil to Hungary and Slovakia have been suspended since 27 January after Kyiv said a Russian drone strike hit pipeline equipment in western Ukraine. Ukraine has proposed alternative transit routes while emergency pipeline repairs continue, including use of its oil transportation system or maritime routes such as the Odesa-Brody pipeline.

citations.quote: Shipments of Russian oil to Hungary and Slovakia have been cut off since 27 January, when Kyiv says a Russian drone strike hit pipeline equipment in western Ukraine.
proposed shipments through Ukraine's oil transportation system or a maritime route, potentially including the Odesa-Brody pipeline linking Ukraine's main Black Sea port to the EU.

segments.heading: Threats to electricity supplies and wider political pressure
segments.text: Slovakia's prime minister said he would ask the state electricity operator to stop emergency electricity supplies to Ukraine if oil transit was not resumed, and Hungary has made similar threats. The two countries have been significant providers of emergency electricity exports to Ukraine. Separately, Hungary has reportedly also threatened to block a large Ukrainian war loan, a move Kyiv cited alongside the energy dispute.

citations.quote: If oil supplies to Slovakia are not resumed on Monday, I will ask SEPS, the state-owned joint-stock company, to stop emergency electricity supplies to Ukraine
Between them, Hungary and Slovakia have been providing around half of European emergency electricity exports to Ukraine

Hungary has also threatened to block a 
c90bn Ukrainian war loan.

segments.heading: Security context and energy infrastructure damage
segments.text: The dispute comes as Ukraine's energy system has been subject to intensified drone and missile attacks in recent months, which Kyiv says have damaged infrastructure and caused widespread blackouts. Separately, there were reports of explosions and air-defence activity in and around Kyiv and attacks in other Ukrainian cities in the same reporting period.

citations.quote: Since October last year, Russia has intensified its drone and missile attacks on the Ukrainian energy system, knocking out electricity and heat and plunging millions of Ukrainians into long blackouts during bitterly cold winter temperatures.
Powerful explosions shook Ukraine's capital Kyiv early on Sunday, after officials warned of a possible ballistic missile attack and ordered residents into shelters.

story.heading: Willie Colón, Pioneering Salsa Trombonist and Composer, Dies at 75
story.summary: Willie Colón, a Bronx-born trombonist, vocalist and composer who helped shape salsa music and sold millions of records, died at 75, his family said. Colón recorded landmark albums including El Malo and Siembra, received industry honors and was active in Latino social causes.

segments.heading: Announcement of death
segments.text: Willie Colón died on Saturday at age 75, according to a family statement posted on his Facebook page. The family asked for privacy as they mourned. The cause of death was not disclosed in the statements released.

citations.quote: "While we grieve his absence, we also rejoice in the timeless gift of his music and the cherished memories he created that will live on forever,"
"He passed away peacefully this morning surrounded by his beloved family."

segments.heading: Early life and musical style
segments.text: Born in the Bronx to Puerto Rican parents, Colón signed with Fania Records as a teenager and released his first album, El Malo, in 1967. His work blended jazz, rock and salsa and drew on rhythms from Cuba, Puerto Rico, Brazil and Africa; critics and biographers have noted recurring themes in his music about Puerto Rican identity and life in the United States.

citations.quote: "Born in the Bronx to Puerto Rican parents"
"Colón’s music combined elements of jazz, rock and salsa, incorporating the rhythms of traditional music from Cuba, Puerto Rico, Brazil, and Africa,"

segments.heading: Notable recordings and commercial success
segments.text: Colón recorded dozens of albums across his career, including La Gran Fuga (1970), El Juicio (1972) and El Malo (1967). His 1978 collaboration with Rubén Blades, Siembra, which includes the song "Pedro Navaja," has been described as the biggest-selling salsa album of all time. Media accounts attribute tens of millions of album sales and multiple industry nominations and awards to Colón.

citations.quote: "His 1978 album Siembra, recorded with Rubén Blades, includes the hit Pedro Navaja and remains the biggest-selling salsa album of all time."
"With more than 30m albums sold, multiple platinum records and 11 combined Grammy and Latin Grammy nominations, Colón is among the most successful salsa artists of all time."

segments.heading: Recognition and public roles
segments.text: Colón received industry honors over his career, including a 2004 lifetime achievement award from the Latin Academy of Recording Arts and Sciences. He was also inducted into halls of fame and recognized by outlets and industry groups for his influence on Latin music.

citations.quote: "In 2004, he received a lifetime achievement award from the Latin Academy of Recording Arts and Sciences."
"Billboard magazine named the musician as one of the 30 most influential Latin artists of all time in 2015."

segments.heading: Influence, reactions and activism
segments.text: Colón was widely credited by colleagues and his record label with expanding the reach and scope of salsa music. Managers and industry figures described his work as politically engaged and influential. He also participated in civic and health organizations, serving on bodies that addressed Latino issues and immigrant concerns.

citations.quote: "Willie didn't just change salsa. He expanded it, politicized it, clothed it in urban chronicles and took it to stages where it hadn't been before."
"A longtime social activist, Colón was a member of the Latino Commission on AIDS and the United Nations Immigrant Foundation and was a board member at the Congressional Hispanic Caucus Institute,"
